summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the applicant father was coercive and aggressive toward the respondent mother – Where the applicant was intimidating and aggressive to the children’s treating medical practitioners – Where the applicant is affected by significant psychiatric and neurodevelopmental conditions – Where communication with the applicant will expose the children to adult conflict or distressing narratives – Where the children will spend no time nor have communication with the applicant. FAMILY LAW– COSTS – Where the applicant should pay the respondent’s costs of preparation for trial and appearance at final hearing.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

This application was listed for final hearing on 13 October 2025. On that day the applicant failed to appear. The respondent appeared by counsel, as did the independent children’s lawyer.
The application proceeded on the respondent’s evidence and submissions. At the conclusion of submissions, I pronounced final orders (in the terms set out at the commencement of these reasons) and reserved delivery of my reasons to a later date. The respondent subsequently applied for costs. These are my reasons for making the parenting orders pronounced on 13 October 2025 and the orders for costs that I now make.
The proceedings concern the parties’ three children. Pursuant to interim parenting orders made on 20 June 2025, the children live with the respondent, their mother, and they spend no time with the applicant, their father. The respondent has the sole obligation to make all decisions relating to major long-term issues for the children. On a final basis, she seeks that those orders continue, although in the slightly modified form set out in her further amended Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 25 July 2025.
The independent children's lawyer also supports those orders being made on a final basis.
In his fourth Amended Initiating Application filed on 9 October 2024, the applicant sought final orders for sole parental responsibility for the children and that they live with him and spend no time with the respondent. He sought injunctions for the personal protection of he and the children against the respondent.
Orders were made for the preparation of the application for final hearing. Although the applicant filed an affidavit of evidence in chief on 22 September 2025, that affidavit was filed after the time limited for filing by an order made by another judge. The consequence of the late filing was that the applicant’s initiating proceedings were dismissed and the trial would be conducted on the respondent’s material alone (see order 2 made on 6 August 2025). Nonetheless, in the course of submissions before me at the final hearing, counsel for the independent children’s lawyer tendered the applicant’s affidavit of evidence in chief on 19 September 2025 (it became Exhibit 2) so that she could rely upon certain parts of it to make good her submissions. It is tolerably clear from the content of that affidavit that, at least as at the date of its filing, the applicant intended to pursue the relief set out in his fourth Amended Initiating Application.

BACKGROUND

Given the failure of the applicant to attend for the hearing, only a brief consideration of the relevant background and facts are necessary.
The parties commenced cohabitation in April 2017. They married in 2018. X, the first of the parties’ children was born in 2020. Y was next born in 2021. The parties’ third child, Z, was born in 2024. The parties separated in January 2024. X suffers from a medical condition, a painful and disabling condition affecting his right hip joint.
The respondent relied upon evidence from herself, Ms V, Dr G and Ms D.
Ms V is the Nominated Supervisor and Campus Director at H Centre, the children’s daycare provider. She gave written evidence about X and Y’s attendance at the centre. More importantly, she gave evidence of her dealings with the applicant, including the disruptive and threatening behaviour in which he engaged at the centre.
Ms D is a physiotherapist specialising in paediatric orthopaedics. She has been treating X since 27 January 2023 in connection with the medical condition in his right hip. She also gave evidence about the intimidating and intrusive communications she received from the applicant between 2024 – 2025.
Dr G is an orthopaedic surgeon. X has been under Dr G’s care since January 2023. Dr G gave written evidence about X’s condition and care. Dr G also gave evidence about the applicant’s behaviour towards him and his staff following the applicant’s separation from the respondent. He described that behaviour as rude, inappropriate and intimidating. He described the impediments to X’s timely treatment caused by the applicant’s behaviour. He described the arrangements put in place to try to protect the respondent and X when he had an injection. He describes the subsequent threats and harassment by the applicant following that procedure.
The evidence demonstrates that around the time of the parties’ separation, the applicant experienced acute mental health difficulties and was hospitalised under mental health legislation. He disputes the description of him or his behaviour at that time.
The children spent unsupervised time with the applicant following separation, but it soon moved to supervised time on account of his difficult and erratic behaviour. Ultimately it was suspended because the service provider terminated its service for reasons associated with the applicant’s behaviour.
There have been multiple apprehended domestic violence orders made to protect the respondent and the children against the applicant. The applicant has pleaded guilty to multiple breaches of these orders, resulting in arrests and periods of incarceration.
The evidence demonstrates disagreement about the management of X’s condition and it has been a major source of parental conflict, particularly arising from the applicant’s interactions with various medical practitioners. The applicant has expressed grave concerns about the respondent’s care of the children more generally. He spoke at length about these concerns with Dr E, a Child, Family & Adult Psychiatrist who prepared a report as a single expert about each of the parties. Dr E noted significant matters of concern following his examination of the applicant. He had no such concerns following his examination of the respondent.
The applicant has breached interim parenting orders, including attending the children’s daycare, causing a lockdown and inducing police involvement. Ms V gave clear evidence of these events.
The respondent alleges coercive and aggressive conduct by the applicant; but he denies those allegations. There is substantial evidence to demonstrate that the respondent’s allegations are well made.

CONSIDERATION

On the material, both parents seek sole parental responsibility. The independent children's lawyer submits, and I agree, that joint parental responsibility is not likely to work and will not be in the best interests of these children.
The applicant is not a candidate for sole parental responsibility because his anti-social behaviour (leading to the necessity for multiple apprehended domestic violence orders and the subsequent contravention of some of them) and his non-compliance with orders of this court do not demonstrate a capacity to make decisions that are likely to be in the best interests of these children. Moreover, the evidence demonstrates that he has been obstructive and threatening of medical providers, which has disrupted the children’s, but particularly X’s, access to treatment.
Both the independent children's lawyer and the respondent submit that effective joint decision‑making will be impossible. I accept that submission. The respondent should have sole parental responsibility for the children without any obligation to consult the applicant.
The expert evidence demonstrates significant psychiatric and neurodevelopmental conditions on the applicant’s part. He denies having any psychiatric illness and disputes the legitimacy of his hospitalisation and records. He has little insight into his conditions and that he ceased part of his prescribed psychiatric medication demonstrates this.
The evidence relied upon demonstrates that the applicant is impulsive and is given to incidents of violent and self‑harming behaviour. Some of it has occurred in the presence of the children. The respondent’s evidence is that he has physically assaulted her.
The independent children's lawyer submits that given the applicant has demonstrated and repeated breaches of apprehended domestic violence orders and parenting orders undermines confidence in his ability to comply with future protective arrangements. I agree. Moreover, attempts to manage the risk presented by the applicant in the past have met with little success. Professional supervision was unsuccessful and ultimately terminated.
The independent children's lawyer argues that communication between the children and the applicant will expose them to adult conflict or distressing narratives about the applicant.
The children are very young, but there is credible evidence that X has expressed distress about spending time with the applicant. The evidence is that he exhibited physical and emotional symptoms demonstrative of his distress and anxiety before supervised visits.
The respondent represents a constant in the children’s lives. I am satisfied on the evidence that she has the capacity to meet their intellectual, developmental, psychological and emotional needs. The arrangements for the children following the parties’ separation bespeak an understanding on her part of the importance to these boys of a proper relationship with the applicant, but only if it is safe for that relationship to be advanced.
Despite the applicant’s conduct towards her and others, the respondent has provided and maintained some stability for the children, both on a day-to-day basis and in respect of other aspects of their lives such as X’s health care. But the evidence shows that her capacity to care for the children is, not surprisingly, compromised by the applicant’s behaviour, both towards her and others.
Dr E’s evidence raises serious concerns about the applicant’s capacity to care for himself, let alone the children. He is in no position psychiatrically to share parental responsibility for the children.
Evidence of the respondent’s understanding of the children’s needs is the fact that she has maintained their relationships with extended paternal family members.

CONCLUSION

All of these matters point inexorably to the conclusion that the present interim orders should become the final orders with the very slight modifications suggested by the respondent. The children should live with her, spend no time with the applicant and the respondent should have sole parental responsibility for long-term decision making for them. There should be a protective order pursuant to s 68B of the Act as the respondent proposes.

COSTS

By her Application in a Proceeding filed on 27 October 2025, the respondent seeks an order that the applicant pay her costs of and incidental to the application for parenting orders on an indemnity basis and fixed in the sum of $42,760.44, or alternatively fixed pursuant to Schedule 3 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) in the amount of $34,963.62. She also seeks the costs of the costs application in the sum of $3,432.00 (on an indemnity basis) or alternatively in the sum of $3,126.30 (according to scale). Further she seeks an order for the costs of a specified directions hearing in the sum of $275.00.
The applicant has filed no response to the application for costs.
The respondent submits that there are justifying circumstances for the purposes of s 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) thereby enlivening the discretion to make an order for costs and warranting a departure from the usual rule that parties bear their own costs. She argues that those circumstances also justify an indemnity costs order.
She points out that the applicant failed, without explanation, to attend the hearing on 13 October 2025 (which had been listed for five days). As it was, it was finalised on the first day, saving, no doubt, significant cost. There is no explanation for the applicant’s failure to attend, but given the psychiatric evidence, his absence was perhaps not surprising.
The respondent also submits that the applicant’s conduct in the proceedings has caused her to incur more costs than she otherwise have might. Indeed, his late filed affidavit ran to some 134 pages of deposition and a significant number of exhibits (running at least twice through the alphabet and onto “exhibit ccc”). Of course, given the late filing of that affidavit and the fact that there was a self-executing order providing for the application to be dealt with by way of default, the respondent and her advisors might have chosen to ignore that affidavit, but I accept that to do so would have been fraught.
The respondent contends that the applicant was wholly unsuccessful, as final orders were made substantially in accordance with the respondent’s proposed orders and supported by the independent children’s lawyer. I accept that submission.
The financial statement filed by the respondent in support of this application demonstrates that the respondent has limited financial means. I accept that she supports the children with minimal contribution from the applicant and has incurred significant legal costs (funded by a family loan).
The respondent submits that the proceedings and the incurring of trial costs, were necessitated by the applicant’s failure to properly engage with the process and comply with orders, culminating in his non‑attendance at trial. However, I do not accept this submission. If the applicant had engaged with the trial process, then the respondent’s costs would have increased with no certainly that she would receive a costs order to cover them.
Nor do I accept the respondent’s submission that the applicant’s conduct made it practically impossible for the respondent to make a meaningful written offer of settlement. There was no impediment to her doing so. It may not have been accepted, but that is a different matter.
The respondent argues that not only is a costs order justified, but an indemnity costs order is also justified. She points out that the applicant pursued unmeritorious litigation, wilfully disregarded court processes and conducted himself such that he caused a significant waste of time and money.
I accept that a costs order is appropriate in the circumstances of this case. I find that there are justifying circumstances such that the discretion to make an order for costs is enlivened. However, I do not consider that the case is so exceptional as to warrant an indemnity costs order. The applicant was perfectly entitled to pursue his application, especially in circumstances where the children were spending no time with him. His failure to attend the trial meant that the amount incurred by the respondent was less than it otherwise might have been. There is no explanation as to why the applicant did not appear and given the evidence before the court as to his psychiatric condition, I am not prepared to draw an adverse inference against him about that.
The amount claimed by the respondent according to the scale for costs seems entirely reasonable given the volume of the material. She should have her costs of the final hearing and preparation therefore fixed in the sum of $34,963.62. She should also have her costs of this costs application at scale rates. Her claim in that regard is also entirely reasonable and I will allow those costs at $3,126.30. So too, is her claim for the costs incurred at the directions hearing held on 6 September 2025 which was necessary because of the applicant’s failure to meet earlier directions. The amount in that regard is a reasonable $275.00.
There will be orders accordingly.
